Name: 2023 Merced NWR - Deadman Wetland Ditch
Note: There were some nesting red-winged blackbirds on the outskirts of the colony, some close to the recorder. My first visit to the colony on May 12 I saw lots of nest building activity and heard lots of calls, however when I set the recorder on May 23 the colony was very quiet. I still saw a few birds going in and out of the milkthistle but not nearly as many. I saw a few tricolors bringing insects into the milkthistle on June 1 and June 9, but less than 10 birds. On June 16 I entered the colony (appeared abandoned) and it looked like a lot of the nests were unused, lots of the milkthistle had fallen over in the wind or desiccated, with quite a few nests on the ground. A few nests had evidence of chicks (egg shells, feathers, poo). I didn't see any fledglings nearby or in the vicinity.

Name: 2023 Merced NWR - Deadman Wetland Ditch
Note: There were about 200 birds nest building during the first visit. It dwindled to less than 10 birds by June 1. I counted ~150 nests after the colony abandoned.

Name: 2023 San Luis NWR - Fiddleneck Lake
Note: The birds were nesting in a patch of milkthistle and mustard growing along the levee sides. An earthen canal near the levee had water May- June 22. The recorder was placed south of the colony near the canal. The colony was located near the edge of a dry seasonal wetland.

Name: 2023 San Luis NWR - Fiddleneck Lake
Note: During the nest building stage, I estimated around 500 birds. Most of the colony abandoned in June, I counted 40 tricolors on June 21.

Name: 2023 San Luis NWR - Fiddleneck Lake
Note: There were some red-winged blackbirds nesting near the recorder on the outskirts of the colony. On May 26 the colony was very active/noisy, I saw one tricolor bringing grass for nest building. June 9 the colony was very quiet, I saw a few males and 1 female present. June 14 I saw 40 tricolors, 1 seen bringing insects into the milkthistle. The milkthistle in June was very shriveled and breaking apart, by June 21 lots of the milkthistle had fallen over. June 29 I saw 5 tricolors, 2 carrying insects into the colony. I did not see any fledglings on any subsequent visits.

Name: 2023 Merced NWR - Cottonwood Trail
Note: I placed the recorder very close to the colony, alongside the milkthistle in some tall grass. The colony is in milkthistle patches alongside a canal ditch that had water. There was some mugwort along the canal as well.

Name: 2023 Merced NWR - Cottonwood Trail
Note: early estimates were close to 2,500. Many birds abandoned to 300 and near the end there were only 10 nests left.

Name: 2023 Merced NWR - Cottonwood Trail
Note: colony may have fledged a few, but not many based on site visits. I saw that lots of the milkthistle had fallen over (7ft tall) when it started drying out. Some nests looked like they had evidence of chicks.

Name: 2023 San Luis NWR - Tanager Slough
Note: The birds nested in the willows and buttonbush in the river channel as well as the milkthistle along the levee. The birds in the milkthistle abandoned early (June 20) after the milkthistle dried up and fell over. I placed the recorder under some of the willow trees in mud near the water, I think the nests closest to the recorder abandoned. The colony successfully fledged chicks.

Name: 2023 San Luis NWR - Tanager Slough
Note: during nestbuilding (June 15-21) there were 800-900 birds. Seemed to drop off to 200 birds during incubation. Saw around 60 fledglings.

Name: 2024 Merced NWR - Duck Slough Farmfield
Note: Birds were nesting in a weedy field (mustard and milk thistle) next to an almond orchard. BIrds were travelling from the nearby dairies to the field. There were seasonal wetlands near the colony that the birds also used for foraging and sources of mud. Corn was planted near the colony, about 60 meters away during the beginning of the nesting attempts. The recorder was placed on the side of the colony, near the densest cluster.

Name: 2018 Yankee Slough Ranch Preserve
Note:
Your google map is the "Yankee Slough" site where we banded in 2018. This site did fail in 2018. It started out around 3000 and eventually failed.  Very few fledglings observed. Less than 10.  Regarding your map for this site, the nest area stopped before the large loop on your map but extended about 200 feet north. So, it would be just the narrow section between the wide areas. See map below.  No tricolors here in 2019.
There were no tricolors at the "Red Wing Ranch" site this year (2019).  We did not monitor this site in 2018 but based on nests located in September (2018), after the breeding season, the nest area ran from the bridge (left marker) east to property line (right marker).  Of approximately 2300 feet, 1400 feet was used for nesting.  
I attached google maps showing the length of the nest area used at each site.
Hope this helps.
Deren

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: Continuing to feed young, with about 30 fledglings visible on the blackberries. A total of 450 adults still feeding young or hanging out near the colony structure. Final colony estimate of 2500 adult birds. The largest stable colony.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: No activity. Not sure what to say about this one. Had 160 birds in the area the first week, and then mostly 35 to 40, and two weeks ago, only 12 birds, but they were taking food to blackberries. They could have fledged or abandoned. Because feeding was observed, I think an estimate of 40 adults is reasonable.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 2500 birds. Continuing to feed young. About 20 fledglings visible. Some young still in nests as adults with food are going deep into berries. More evidence that the activity of this colony is stretched out, with some birds initiating nesting at least two weeks before others.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: No activity. There were only 12 birds seen last week, but they were taking food to blackberries. They could have fledged or abandoned.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 2500 birds. It was windy, so the conditions weren't ideal, but there was a lot of feeding and about a dozen fledglings were visible.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: 12 birds. Distant, but even with a scope I couldn't see much. Saw a few females collecting food and delivering it to the colony site. Perhaps there are more birds incubating, but there is hardly any activity since the first week of 160 birds. Only two males seen.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 2500 birds. A lot of feeding but still some nest building and courting. Definitely more than one stage going. Will see if there are any fledglings this coming Friday.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: 9 birds. Distant, but even with a scope I couldn't see much. Maybe next week it will be clearer what's going on. Only two males and seven females seen, but females went into the blackberries that have been the focus of activity for the past four weeks. Possibly incubating. Will see.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 2500 birds. Back to full strength. Very actively nest-building, which is a surprise as there was some nest building two weeks ago and I figured they would be much farther along now. Either the first effort failed, was an early wave, or they're just getting revved up now. Will follow up next week.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: 35 birds (a slight increase). Distant, but even with a scope I couldn't see many birds. Active, but barely apparent.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 800 birds (down from 2500 birds last week). It was much more active last week and I suspect they are incubating right now.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: 18 birds (down from 160 birds last week). A little distant, but even with a scope I couldn't see many birds. They may be incubating. Will see next week.

Name: 2021 Orchard Creek
Note: 2500 birds. It was much more active when I revisited it in the afternoon and my estimate went up from around 1200 to 2500. Just about to get started. Lots of singing, saw copulating, one female with nesting material (but only one). The rest of the time they foraged and then landed back on the Himalayan blackberry, with some females venturing inside.

Name: 2021 Rodeo Grounds
Note: 160 birds. A little distant, but with a scope could make out some nest building. Appeared to be a fairly small colony, but we'll see. New development going in pretty close to the colony site, but the birds seemed to have quite a bit of open space left to forage in.

Name: 2024 San Jacinto Wildlife Area - Thistle Patch NW of Bridge Street Pond
Note: The San Jacinto Wildlife Area continues to vex me and the birds.  As you will find in the recorder, the colony there was small and late, due directly to the  actions of Scott Sewell the manager.  Nobody in his CDFW hierarchy seems to have done anything to exert their influence to overrule his relegation of Tricolored Blackbirds to a species of little concern.  I know you avoid politics to maintain your scientific impartiality, so I tell you this simply as a matter of facts, to help explain the decline in tricolors this year.  The preferred nesting marsh was disked in the winter of 23-24, and did not have sufficient growth to support a colony this season. Last year's  colony site in the San Jacinto River channel was never irrigated, first due to a broken water line, but after it was fixed, no water was relegated to the site.  Lastly, the spring/summer wetland, which intermittently has supported tricolors and was established for their benefit, was never irrigated, despite no problems with the pumps or lines.

Name: 2024 Newberry Springs  - Lake Wainani
Note: The Newberry Springs colony is still hanging on by a toenail, with two new colony sites that became suitable after the increased expense of water led to the owners dropping the water level and forgoing cattail maintenance. There was no alfalfa grown in Newberry Springs, only 3 Way hay in the spring plus bermudagrass. Last years pond was dry and abandoned. It’s a matter of a very few years until they are gone.

Name: 2024 San Jacinto Wildlife Area - Thistle Patch NW of Bridge Street Pond
Note: Hello Scott et al., I placed the recording unit today near the shore of Mystic Lake near the thistle colony.  So far, production appears to be very low, with only about 20 juveniles fledged so far, but there appears to be newer activity at the other end of the thistle patch.  Food is coming from the dairy as it did in 2022.  The relatively cool weather will hopefully help this late nesting to be successful.  Dave

Name: 2024 Dagnino Pond
Note:
I noted mating behavior including copulation on May 15.  On May 15 the colony was also very noisy and active, with several tens of mostly male birds doing the “conveyor belt” foraging behavior.  I saw one bird on a nest.

Name: 2024 Panoche Creek Marsh East
Note:
Hi Wendy, here is the location info for our three PVP AMs from 2024:

AM#06 South- 36.589212'N 120.770691'W
AM#25 West- 36.591868'N 120.772323'W
AM#42 East- 36.590464'N 120.769368'W

I attached a photo of the "Dollar Marsh" area where TRBL nested in 2024. This area remains the largest stronghold of cattails along the PVP portion of Panoche Creek. When I deployed the AMs, TRBL were well distributed among the cattails throughout this entire area. As nesting began in earnest, it appeared most of the birds(1000-1500) were concentrated in the area directly surrounding AM#42-EAST. There was another group(several hundred) across the marsh adjacent to AM#06-SOUTH. The TRBL using the patchy cattails south of AM#26WEST did not stick around as I thought they might, and I have little hope they were able to successfully nest in that area.

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Wetland across Broadstone from Vista Del Lago High School, Checklist S54157162, Count:650, 3-5 groups of birds came in, perched in trees, swirled around numerous times and most then roosted down inside old cattails in detention pond. 95% of birds were male tricolors, with about 5% females and a few Red-wings. Surrounding area mostly developed, so I doubt birds would nest here.

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: 101–151 Healthy Way, Folsom US-CA (38.6463,-121.1044), Checklist S55374168, Count:1500, Approximately 200 males (occasional singing). The rest were female who were singing a different song. Most of the females were hidden from view in a blackberry patch where they are nesting. It appears that nest building may be complete.

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Iron Point Rd, Checklist S54638983, Count:70, Singing very infrequently, which seemed surprising to me. Males perched at top of blackberry. Coming and going in flocks of 5-35 from area to the south. Counted sex ration was 22M:27F. Nesting is not occurring yet.

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Iron Point Rd, Checklist S55452014, Count:2300, Colony appears to be in incubation phase

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Iron Point Rd, Checklist S55074096, Count:1200, At traditional breeding colony, which was abandoned during construction last year. Males singing. No nest building seen

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Hwy 50 East of Bidwell, Checklist S56534788, Count:15, The once huge TRBL colonies in this area (25 years ago) are gone. A remnant group of commuting birds was seen flying over Hwy 50 (N to S) around the edge of the Lifetime Fitness building. Considerable development ongoing around the area.

Name: 2019 Iron Point
Note: Iron Point Rd, Checklist S56058834, Count:6000, This colony grew with the apparent arrival of a large number of birds that are now singing and nest-building. The estimate is based on a count of 2160 birds (mostly males) leaving the site, the size of the blackberry patch (which has grown since last year), number of uncounted birds estimated to be at the colony, and knowledge that birds are travelling 1-4 km to feed, so many were likely away during count. So if anything, this is an underestimate

Name: 2021 Sedgwick Reserve Pond
Note: Sedgwick Reserve (restricted access), Checklist S82864879, Count:600, Arrived prior to dawn to observe morning fly-out. Noted song from males, white patch on male wings, and pattern of flocking behavior to identify them as TRBL.--First fly-out was at 6:24. Observed 2 groups flying out that we estimated at about 100 each and 3 groups of about 50, flying out in rapid succession (maybe 10-20 secs between fly-outs). About 10 min later there were 6 more groups of from 20-50 birds each, again in rapid succession. After that we counted another 30 or so males remaining in the reeds and singing. Total number was somewhere around 600.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--vineyard (restricted access), Checklist S105130919, Count:3, becoming increasingly common to hear and spot

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S106413027, Count:1800, Clearly, the most numerous bird species at this location. Both males and females present. My best estimate at this time, counting by 100's. However, there may well be more birds present than 1800. They were very, very vocal. Flying in and out of the roosting spots in the cattails/tules surrounding the pond. We had seen them earlier, in flocks flying over the vineyard. In fact, I found a flock at the farthest reaches of the ranch property about 2.6 miles from this pond. I did not see any individuals carrying nesting material or feeding young. I only saw one male doing breeding display. However, with much warmer weather forecast for the next 3 consecutive days, full courtship may ensue. I made some photographs of the pond, primarily to show the vegetation. Much of the cattail/tule veg is brown, and presumably not suitable for nesting. Years ago, Robert Meese visited the ranch and this pond, specifically. He said the veg needed to be burned. It was later burned, many thanks to the ranch owner and Kelly Mulville. Crossing my fingers that nesting will take place this spring. This pond is fenced and it should be easy to protect. Number of males vs females is a very rough estimate.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S106749524, Count:2000, 2000 is my best estimate, could be plus or minus 200. After only a few minutes, I saw a female carrying straw-colored vegetation about 18 inches long. I counted at least 65 females carrying nesting material. Some other materials were bright green or straw-colored and only 5 inches long, or less. I saw one bird carrying nest material that looked like it was a bit muddy. I never entered the fenced area inside of the pond because the blackbirds were foraging for nesting material inside of the fenced area as well as flying out to other areas. No human, nor any animals should be permitted inside of the pond fencing. In fact, all activity should be confined to the road only. I deployed the Audio Moth per Bob Meese's instructions. I hope it works!

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S107399582, Count:900, The colony has reached the incubation stage where the birds are very, very quiet. Females are presumably incubating eggs now. I saw streaming flocks flying out, mostly to the east/southeast. Flying flocks included some females. Presumably, it was warm enough for them to leave their eggs since it was mid-day.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S108225896, Count:1200, Very quiet at the colony. Numerous individuals carrying food to the nests, most flying to the east-southeast. Bob and I tried to find where they were landing on the ground to feed. We drove to the farthest reaches of the ranch in that direction, but the blackbirds were not going down in those paddocks/fields. I wonder if they might be flying all the way to Browns Valley Road. I will try to spend some time out there soon. A workshop was happening at the ranch during our visit. About 40+ folks walked along the road past the breeding colony without any reaction on the part of the birds. Ranch life seems to not impact this colony as long as no one enters the fencing around the pond. Bob agreed with me that the plant structure is less than optimum per Tricolored Blackbird requirements. Nevertheless they are using it this season.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--entrance lane pepper trees (restricted access), Checklist S106838214, Count:500, in a streaming flock, heading east-southeast.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--entrance lane pepper trees (restricted access), Checklist S106750892, Count:110, flock heading southeast, as was the flock at the reservoir.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--vineyard (restricted access), Checklist S106752142, Count:660, Counted by 10's in 8 different flocks ranging from as low as 20 in a flock to 200.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S106838151, Count:2800, Today, I saw fewer birds carrying nesting material, but I also saw several pairs copulating. I upped the numbers from 2000 to 2800 because I was able to count the flocks flying out, almost in a continuous stream, all heading toward the east-southeast. After checking the vineyards (after I left the pond) for flocks, and finding none, I encountered a good sized flock, heading east-southeast over the vineyard on the east side of Highway 25. Fortunately, no egrets at the pond today.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S107027870, Count:3000, Getting quieter at the pond. The sounds of the Red-wings are quite noticeable now. Long streams of trikes were flying from the pond to the east-southeast, many in the field where the sheep/cattle are grazing. Sometimes the flocks were high in the sky, but as sunset approached, flocks were in long streams just barely above the vegetation. I counted one stream of 1800 birds, counting by 100's. That, plus the number of birds that I thought were already present at the pond lead me to a count of 3000. There might be as many as 4000 trikes present. I saw no birds carrying food (indicating feeding young), and only one individual carrying nesting material (a late nester?). I saw only a handful of females displaying and only one or two males displaying. I'm not sure if they are waiting out for warmer weather, or if they really plan to nest, or not. It seems as though I should have seen far more females building nests on my last visit. OR, I may have missed the peak of the nest construction between 5 April to 10 April. Time will tell. Crossing my fingers for a successful colony at this location.

Name: 2022 Paicines Ranch - Teal Pond
Note: Paicines Ranch--Teal Pond (restricted access), Checklist S117292455, Count:1, Remarkably, the solitary male circled about five times over the "pond" before landing in the tules. Most trikes have departed from San Benito County for the the Delta, etc., according to Bob Meese. I haven't seen any trikes in San Benito County for a couple of months. Bob Messe wrote to me: "This is the time of year when the birds can be most difficult to locate, and when they're found in large flocks on paddies of ripening rice in the upper Sacramento Valley, and then remain there until early November, when they move back down into the Delta and coastal locations. I have not looked at eBird for awhile, but it's not surprising that they're tough to find as they're often far from public roads, in areas where few birders seek birds as the landscape is dominated by rice." He is studying these late fall roost areas.

Name: 2022 Washington State - McCain Wetands
Note: Para/McCain's Ponds, Checklist S111256598, Count:74, Private property. Min count at one time, colony on private lands. 31 male high counted at once. Many others feeding away from colony so at least 100 adults, but could be many more. Rwbl not in vicinity of colony but present at wetlands. YHbl near colony, at perimeter mostly. After counting, I noticed a satellite cluster of 6 males that are not included, so maybe another group of adults breeding there. Both males and females perching up and going to forage when I first arrived, which might have been related to my presence as later mostly males perched up and females were mostly in veg (presume at nests) Females carrying food when returning to colony but they return quickly down into veg so hard to see what they have. One female returned and fed another bird that I realized was a fledgling. One female left veg with a fecal sac and flew over the water and dropped it. Most nests must be with young, only a few advanced w fledglings. Unable to see any nests. Males returning to colony did not seem to bring food but some dropped into veg so maybe also some male provisioning at nestling stage?

Name: 2023 Iron Point

Name: 2023 Iron Point
Note: Iron Point Rd, Checklist S136476584, Count:400, Surprised to find roughly 300-500 here at colony site again after a year’s absence. The nesting area is the Himalayan Blackberry area used in the past, about half of which is dead. Appear to be at the egg laying stage as are not vocalizing and females were out and about. Birds were flying to feed to the southeast instead of their usual former route south and southwest. A large 3 story building is under construction immediately adjacent to the colony site. There is a lot more catalyst growing here upstream of the blackberry patch, but most of it is narrow and birds were not using it. Most of the adjacent foraging habitat south of US 50 is developed or under active development, so I wonder where they are foraging? I did not see any along Payen Rd.

Name: 2023 Oregon State - Warm Springs Dizney Lane

Name: 2023 Oregon State - Warm Springs Dizney Lane
Note: Northwest Dizney Lane, Madras, Oregon, US (44.723, -121.238), Checklist S136003129, Count:210, Minimum estimate, possibly as many as 350. Several flocks of 50-100 continuously moving back and forth between pond and adjacent pasture. Minimum count accomplished by observing three separate flocks arrive at pond in short succession. Distinguished from Red- winged Blackbirds by bold white epaulet border on males, sharper, more acute bills, and distinctive vocalizations.

Name: 2023 Panoche Creek Preserve - Corrals North West
Note: Panoche Valley Preserve-fiddlehead Tric colony-2023, Checklist S135411666, Count:2000, Ben Teton discovered this apparent Tricolored Blackbird colony. It appears as if they are going to nest, or have already built nests deep in the fiddlehead flowers. Hard to say. Their usual habitat along Panoche Creek was flattened by flooding this winter. The fiddlehead plants are dense and over 2 feet tall. Other plants are mixed in with them. Nests could be deep inside. We could not see any nests without disturbing the colony which we did not do. About 1000 tricolors flew up from inside of the fiddlehead plants when we arrived. They were completely silent. Other tricolors were flying around the adjacent area near a solitary mesquite tree. It appeared that they were foraging/hunting food. My lowest count is 2000 tricolors and highest estimate is 2800. I estimated four females for every male. Colony needs protection from grazing cattle, and further study. Ben will deploy audio moths which I will deliver to him on 29 April 2023. Chris Hauser will help with plant ID.

The red on the shoulder (epaulet) visably darker red than on red-winged blackbirds. Also lacked the yellow that a lot of red winged blackbirds have. Bottom of the shoulder patch clearly white. As it became closer to dusk, the blackbirds became more active and came out of hiding from the reeds to perch out in the open more. Also heard doing “angry cat” song between flock members. They became increasingly vocal as evening approached. Have audio recordings and photos that I will attach. Lifer!

Name: 2023 Washington State - Harder Spring
Note: Harder Spring, Checklist S140256110, Count:8, This species is known to have a colony that lives here or is repeatedly reported here, the public has been reporting them since at least April 2022 because the site was opened by the owner to birders then. The male birds are shiny black with red and bright white coverts instead of the red and yellow coverts that the Red-winged Blackbirds have. The Yellow-headed Blackbirds just have white coverts but are easily told apart from both of the other 2 species because they have yellow on the heads of both sexes and the other 2 species have no yellow on the heads of either sex. The birds seen today were not in the reeds at the spring but in one of the pastures or fields across the driveway from the spring with the nearest bird sitting on a fence. The birds would fly from the field towards some trees nearby and then fly back to the field again.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S172386214, Count:50, Very low activity level at colony site. Desultory visits by handfuls of birds. Very little vocalization. Two females observed carrying food. This is very different from two weeks ago and also a month ago when feral pigs were discovered at the site. At these earlier visits, several hundred birds were observed with a cacophony of noise. Still too early for fledglings to have left, so either the entire colony was away on an extended foraging trip or the colony has been largely abandoned. There were several coyotes (at least 5) in the area. Still apparently too cool for the rattlesnakes that have been seen at the site periphery the last couple seasons at this time of year.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S175969868, Count:600, A notable amount of crickets were seen jumping along the path towards the blackberry patch. Flocks of approximately 80 tricolored blackbirds were observed foraging and returning to the patch, with an estimated 600 or more at the site. Activity levels were extremely high and were accompanied by loud vocalizations. A handful of females were seen perched atop the patch with food in their beaks. A flock of nearly 100 birds (primarily males) were observed flying across the water and returning to the bush- 4 males were spotted carrying food and 4 more were carrying twigs. The birds were actively foraging on the ground in the foothills and along the water. There was murmuring from within the blackberry patch as well. Overall, the colony seems to have returned with considerable activity. Four coyotes were spotted in the hills, although they were more distant from the site this time.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S177632162, Count:300, Colony fairly quiet, some singing by males, and other noises. Could be many more birds on nest unseen. Both sexes coming and going, and loafing atop brambles. One female carrying nesting material. No birds seen carrying food. No fledglings seen.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S172445049, Count:40, About 40 in total spotted. A small flock of 10 blackbirds was observed relocating from the blackberry bush to the nearby hills and grassy areas, likely in search of foraging opportunities. Only about 15 birds seen perching atop the bush and a handful seen entering inside. 3 females were noted returning to the bush with food, yet overall, the site appeared quiet. Very little vocalization and it seems that much it the colony has been abandoned. There were 6 coyotes spotted near the site and no rattlesnakes seen. Stayed around the site for 30 minutes and no changes were observed.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S183534075, Count:450, ~150 In oaks about 1/2 mile from colony, Sounded like some begging young. 50 misc. flyovers. ~150 still at colony, many adults carrying grasshoppers, fledglings visible at top of bramble being fed. Counted 30 juvs, probably many more, Some still with down and bare facial area. Asynchronous breeding cycle? Another~100 foraging in dry lakebed. Three coyotes in area, no rattlesnakes observed at colony. Pile of blackbird feathers in grass at colony edge - hawk predation?

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S181512955, Count:300, Tricolor Blackbird colony survey. Known colony in blackberry bramble. Photos of 7 individuals. ~250 coming and going at this colony. Most carrying food (grasshoppers). No juveniles seen, so presumably still in nest out of sight. Another ~50 seen foraging in open field at least a mile away. Only a couple birds seen at suspected second colony in tules approx. 0.5 mi SSW of blackberry bramble. Now seems that this location was a staging area for previous fledges. No evidence of nesting seen at this second site (no guano staining the tules or at the base of the tules). Two coyotes seen in vicinity. One rattlesnake at periphery of blackberry bramble colony.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S180770508, Count:300, Tricolored Blackbird colony survey. Known colony in blackberry bramble - about 200 birds, not too noisy, a lot of females carrying food (plentiful grasshoppers mostly). Just a few males carrying food. Less active than during the last two years. Another suspected colony was discovered in a small patch of Tules about 1/2 mile SSW of the first. Here, about 100 birds. Adults seen carrying food, also witnessed females feeding apparent young on adjacent barbed-wire fence, though these 'young' didn't have the wispy tufts of feathers I've seen before, so may have been fledged for a while. The recording is from the second presumed site, as well as the photo of a bird on a fence wire.

Name: 2024 Los Vaqueros Playa
Note: Los Vaqueros Reservoir--Kellogg Creek Staging Area, Checklist S186384213, Count:75, Continuing. Seen best in two trees southwest of staging area just off Kellogg Creek Trail along creek at 37.845, -121.705. Seen in trees flying to field to forage or roost on fence. a few seen in tree northeast of staging area near bridge over Kellogg creek. Immature and females seem to predominate with a few males clearly marked with dusky red and white coverts. Quite vocal but clearly distinct from musical notes of red-winged blackbird.

Note: Eureka Lane Marsh, Checklist S171804398, Count:550, Tightly packed into a small (<5ha) marsh. A known nesting colony. On males: Thin pure white line bordering red shoulder patch. Name: 2024 Oregon State -  Metolius Eureka Lane
Note: Eureka Lane Marsh, Checklist S171233540, Count:550, Tightly packed into a small (<5ha) marsh. A known nesting colony. On males: Thin pure white line bordering red shoulder patch. Red often not visible at all while the bird is perched. Slightly smaller than nearby RWBL. Song and calls more high-pitched and nasal than RWBL, like a bleating goat, without a distinct "con-quer" intro note. Photos and videos taken. Count was estimated by counting 10 birds at a time up to 50, then estimating the number of groups of 50. Photo P1094166 best shows the large number of birds, with at least 150 being easily counted in that photo alone. That was the densest part of the flock, the rest were spread evenly across the rest of the wetland. As seen in photos, YHBL were not associating with the main part of the flock of TRBL, and were more in the foreground and scattered in a lower density. There was only a small group of RWBL all in the front left corner of the marsh as you face it from the road. They were noticeably larger with more yellow shoulder patches.

Name: 2024 Sedgwick Reserve Pond
Note: Sedgwick Reserve (restricted access), Checklist S164227098, Count:150, [6:08am]. The sounds of TrBl are definitely coming from the northern part of the marsh. --[6:20am] A bunch of tricolors move out of roost mode (buried deep down in the cattails) and they move south in the marsh and co-occupy areas with the RwBl (though the photo shows these are almost exclusively TrBl).--[6:23am] Successive groups of 100 each maybe 400-500 blackbirds in total take off from the marsh and circle overhead and then some TrBl and RwBl remain, but lacking these departing birds, the earlier cacophony has diminished. --[6:39am] There are no sounds of blackbirds coming from the marsh.--Around 9:20am a group of 15 or so males assembled in a tree near the pond while a tour group was standing next to the pond. These birds may have been waiting to re-enter the pond.--I saw no suggestion that nest building might have begun, though that is an activity that might not show in the early morning.

Note: Sedgwick Reserve Pond, Checklist S167105906, Count:5, [06:11am] As in previous visits the vocals of tricolored are from the north end of the pond and Red Wings predominantly from the middle. --6:19 am The sounds of TrBl becomes louder.--[06:40] First set of four photos taken at 6:39 AM 6:40 AM about 40 birds takeoff from Tricolored part of pond. --6:47 AM a single photo of tricolored now in the center part of the pond. --6:47 am I took another photo of those tricolors in the center part of the pond. --6:48 am The tricolors seem to have expanded their area a little bit to the north west and then there are those in the center part of the pond now. --6:50 AM a flock of 50 to 100 closer to 100 now blackbirds have taken off and they seem to be tricolored. --6:51 AM a.m. the another series of four photos starting at the center part of the pond and working northwest. --6:52 another flock of 120 birds tricolors takeoff. --6:53 another flock of 100 tricolors takes off. --Also at 6:53 another flock of about 40 tricolors takes off. So now there is very little tricolored or red wing vocalization. In fact, I haven't heard Red-wings in quite a while. --6:54 single photo was taken off probably won't turn out. --6:56 now there's only a couple of tricolors singing and more than a couple are in the marsh. These are from the north end of the marsh. --6:57 PM there's only one male perched up in the north end of the marsh. Make that a.m.--[07:01] About 60 tricolors return to the pond and sing perched. Make that count more like 50 Of those 50 only about 17 are visible.--[07:09] I'm leaving the pond area at which time there are only about five or so singing tricolored males. As I'm now 50 m south of the pond I'm hearing a lot of tricolored blackbirds singing from about 150 m south east of the pond in the field.--[07:16] At 7:16 AM I'm hearing a few Tricolored blackbirds from 150 m south. I mean I'm hearing them in the pond from my position which is 150 m south.
